rubric.question_to_annotator: Does the model's output still assert the must_persist fact (a fact that should survive the correction unchanged)?
rubric.answer_YES: the fact is present and stated as live (restated in different words but same meaning = YES/kept).
rubric.answer_NO: the fact is absent or contradicted.

instructions: You are validating an AI model's revision behavior. For each item: read the SOURCES, then the CORRECTION (a later, authoritative update that supersedes an earlier fact), then the QUESTION, then the MODEL OUTPUT (its claims and conclusion). Apply the rubric to the SCORED SENTENCE only. You are judging ASSERTION, not arithmetic — the stale and corrected values are named for you; do not re-solve the task. Put YES/NO/UNSURE in label.judgment, your id in label.annotator_id, and any note in label.notes. Do not consult other annotators. You must pass the 6-item calibration set first.

sources.text: Bulk-gas carrier Denwick Fuels is delivering a single tanker load of propane to the Marrowfield depot's bulk storage tank, a fixed one-tanker delivery with no alternative storage tank available while tank 2 remains isolated for valve repair.

The depot's bulk storage tank is filled to a level leaving only 8,000 litres of ullage capacity before the tank's 85-percent fill-limit is reached. The incoming tanker carries a fixed 12,000-litre load of propane. Safety code prohibits offloading a volume exceeding the tank's remaining ullage capacity.

The depot's offload pump is rated for a maximum transfer pressure of 17 bar for this hose-and-coupling configuration. The tanker's onboard compressor is set to push product at 21 bar for a fast offload. The pump's technical data sheet states operating above the rated transfer pressure risks coupling failure and is prohibited.

Site procedure prohibits bulk propane offload when ambient temperature exceeds 38 degrees Celsius, due to elevated vapour-pressure risk in the receiving tank. The depot's site thermometer currently reads 42 degrees Celsius. The site procedure states offloads above the ambient-temperature limit must be held until conditions cool.

correction.text: Inventory update: following a scheduled outload to local retail tanks overnight, the storage tank's remaining ullage capacity is now 13,000 litres, replacing the 8,000-litre figure.

presented.question: Considering the storage tank's remaining ullage capacity, the offload pump's rated maximum transfer pressure, and the site's ambient-temperature offload limit, can the tanker delivery be offloaded as planned, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: Document 4 corrects the earlier statement in document 1 that the tank's remaining ullage capacity is 8,000 litres, updating it to 13,000 litres following a scheduled overnight outload.
The now-corrected remaining ullage capacity is 13,000 litres.
The incoming tanker carries a fixed 12,000-litre load of propane.
Safety code prohibits offloading a volume exceeding the tank's remaining ullage capacity.
Since 12,000 litres is less than the corrected 13,000-litre ullage capacity, the ullage constraint no longer blocks the offload.
The depot's offload pump is rated for a maximum transfer pressure of 17 bar for this hose-and-coupling configuration.
The tanker's onboard compressor is set to push product at 21 bar for a fast offload, exceeding the pump's rated maximum transfer pressure.
Operating above the rated transfer pressure risks coupling failure and is prohibited per the pump's technical data sheet.
Site procedure prohibits bulk propane offload when ambient temperature exceeds 38 degrees Celsius.
The depot's site thermometer currently reads 42 degrees Celsius, exceeding the ambient-temperature limit.

Because the ambient temperature exceeds the limit and the compressor pressure exceeds the pump's rated maximum, two of the three original constraints still prohibit the offload, even though the ullage constraint is now satisfied.

model_output.conclusion: No, the tanker delivery cannot be offloaded as planned: although the corrected 13,000-litre ullage capacity now accommodates the 12,000-litre load, the offload is still stopped by the compressor's 21 bar output exceeding the pump's 17 bar rated maximum transfer pressure and by the ambient temperature of 42°C exceeding the 38°C site procedure limit.

presented.must_persist_fact: The delivery is a fixed one-tanker load of propane to Marrowfield depot's bulk tank.

sources.text: Stage crew Halloway Playhouse is flying a scenic backdrop cloth for tonight's single performance of the touring production, a fixed one-show flying plot with no substitute backdrop available in the venue's stock.

The flying bar assigned to this cloth is rated for a maximum line-set load of 80 kilograms across its counterweight system. The backdrop cloth, battened and dressed, weighs a measured 105 kilograms. The venue's rigging log states flying a load above the rated line-set capacity is not permitted.

The stage's grid requires a minimum clearance of 9 metres above the proscenium arch for the cloth to fly out fully during scene changes. This venue's grid height above the proscenium measures only 7.2 metres. The venue's technical specification states a flying piece cannot clear the grid if the piece's full-out height exceeds available clearance.

Venue fire code requires the fire curtain's interlock system to show a ready-and-tested status before any scenic piece may fly across the proscenium line during a performance. Tonight's pre-show test logs the interlock status as fault, following an unresolved sensor fault from the matinee. Fire code states flying across the proscenium line with a faulted interlock is prohibited.

correction.text: Rigging update: with additional counterweight added to the arbor, the flying bar's rated line-set capacity is now 115 kilograms, replacing the 80-kilogram figure.

presented.prior_corrections_note: This scenario carries 2 stacked corrections; all are in the sources/correction shown. Read them all before judging.
presented.question: Considering the flying bar's rated line-set capacity, the grid's minimum clearance above the proscenium, and the fire-curtain interlock's readiness rule, can the backdrop flying cue proceed as planned, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: The flying bar's original rated line-set capacity of 80 kilograms is superseded by a rigging update setting it to 115 kilograms after additional counterweight was added.
The backdrop cloth weighs 105 kilograms.
Under the corrected 115-kilogram capacity, the 105-kilogram backdrop cloth is within the rated line-set load, so the rigging weight limit does not stop the cue.
The original minimum grid clearance requirement of 9 metres is superseded by a rigging update setting it to 6.5 metres due to a shorter substitute batten configuration.
The venue's actual grid height above the proscenium is 7.2 metres.
Under the corrected 6.5-metre clearance requirement, the 7.2-metre grid height is sufficient, so the grid clearance issue no longer stops the cue.
Fire code requires the fire curtain's interlock system to show a ready-and-tested status before any scenic piece may fly across the proscenium line during a performance.
Tonight's pre-show test logged the interlock status as fault due to an unresolved sensor fault from the matinee.
Fire code prohibits flying across the proscenium line with a faulted interlock, and no document corrects or resolves this fault status.
Because the interlock fault remains unresolved and no substitute backdrop or alternate flying plot exists, the fire curtain interlock rule is the sole remaining obstacle to the flying cue.

model_output.conclusion: The backdrop flying cue cannot proceed as planned: the rigging capacity and grid clearance concerns are resolved by the later updates (115 kg capacity comfortably covers the 105 kg cloth, and 7.2 m clearance exceeds the revised 6.5 m requirement), but the fire curtain interlock is still logged as faulted, and fire code prohibits flying across the proscenium line under that condition, so the faulted interlock alone stops the cue.

presented.must_persist_fact: The flown piece is a scenic backdrop cloth for tonight's single performance.

sources.text: Charter operator Blackmere is planning a single non-stop sector for a light business jet from Enfield Field to Corvale Regional, a fixed great-circle distance of 1,650 nautical miles.

The route's manual states a flight may not be filed non-stop if forecast headwind reduces effective range by more than 150 nautical miles.

Corvale Regional's approach plates require any arriving charter to carry a minimum diversion-fuel reserve of 45 minutes to its designated alternate. The filed flight plan currently carries only 30 minutes of diversion-fuel reserve. The plates state an arrival below the minimum reserve may not be accepted into the approach.

correction.text: Weight-and-fuel update: with a reduced-payload fuel configuration, the aircraft's certified maximum range with reserves is now 1,750 nautical miles, replacing the 1,500 nm figure.

Under the corrected certified maximum range of 1,750 nautical miles, the planned 1,650 nautical mile sector no longer exceeds the certified range.
Because the sector no longer exceeds certified range, the flight manual rule barring non-stop filing when the sector exceeds certified range with reserves does not apply.
Forecast headwind of 45 knots reduces effective still-air range by 220 nautical miles compared to a no-wind flight plan.
The route's manual prohibits non-stop filing if forecast headwind reduces effective range by more than 150 nautical miles.
Since the 220 nautical mile reduction exceeds the 150 nautical mile threshold, the flight may not be filed non-stop due to the headwind rule.

The filed flight plan currently carries only 30 minutes of diversion-fuel reserve, which is below the required minimum.
An arrival below the minimum diversion-fuel reserve may not be accepted into the approach at Corvale Regional.

Even though the corrected range figure removes the range-based obstacle to filing non-stop, the flight is still blocked by both the headwind-based range reduction rule and the insufficient diversion-fuel reserve.

model_output.conclusion: With the corrected certified maximum range of 1,750 nautical miles, the aircraft's range alone no longer prevents a non-stop filing, but the flight still cannot be planned non-stop as filed because the forecast headwind reduces effective range by 220 nautical miles (exceeding the 150 nm limit) and the flight plan's 30-minute diversion-fuel reserve falls short of Corvale Regional's required 45-minute minimum.
